stem: What should a good tool description include?
options: Only the parameter list — the name conveys the rest
Marketing-style language emphasizing the tool's power
What it does, when to use it, when NOT to use it, input formats, what the output contains, and limitations
Internal implementation details like which backend API it calls
rationales: The agent selects tools from descriptions; a bare parameter list omits the judgment cues (when / when not) that prevent misuse.
Persuasive language biases selection without informing it.

The agent's entire basis for choosing and invoking the tool is its name, description, schema, and examples — so the description must cover purpose, applicability, boundaries, formats, outputs, and limits.
Implementation details don't help the model decide when the tool is appropriate.

stem: A fitness app has one tool, `log_workout(type, value, unit)`, where cardio workouts need duration/distance and strength workouts need sets/reps/weight. Logs show frequent invalid combinations. What is the right fix?
options: Add validation that rejects invalid combinations with an error message
Split into `log_cardio_session` and `log_strength_session` so each schema encodes its own required fields
Expand the description to enumerate valid combinations for each type
Make all parameters optional so no combination is invalid
rationales: Post-hoc validation catches mistakes after the model makes them; better schemas prevent them from being expressible.
When operations have interdependent constraints, separate tools make the schema itself encode the distinction — invalid combinations become unrepresentable rather than merely discouraged.
Description prose asks the model to memorize rules the schema could enforce structurally.
That makes every invalid call schema-legal, trading visible failures for silent bad data.

stem: Users refer to projects by name, names are ambiguous, and a delete tool acts on whatever name string the model passes. An agent just deleted the wrong project. What is the correct pattern?
options: Require the model to double-check the name with the user before every delete
Fuzzy-match names and act on the closest match above a similarity threshold
Add an "are you sure?" boolean parameter to the delete tool
Lookup-then-act: a search tool returns candidate IDs with distinguishing metadata; when multiple match, the user picks; the delete tool accepts only unambiguous IDs
rationales: Per-call confirmation prompts add friction everywhere while the interface still permits acting on an ambiguous name.
Fuzzy matching automates the guess that caused the incident.
The model sets that boolean itself — it adds ceremony, not safety.
Resolving which entity via search + user selection, then acting only on stable IDs, removes name ambiguity from the destructive path entirely.

stem: A search tool finds nothing matching the query. What should it return?
options: A successful result with an empty `results` array and a total count of zero
An `isError: true` result saying "no matches found"
Nothing — omit the tool result so the model moves on
A retryable error so the agent tries alternative phrasings
rationales: "No matches" is a valid answer, not a failure. A successful empty result tells the agent the query worked and the data simply isn't there.
Flagging it as an error invites the agent to retry a perfectly valid query as though the tool broke.
Tool calls must have results; omission is a protocol violation.
Encouraging retries conflates "absent" with "failed" — the classic empty-vs-error confusion.

stem: A document search tool returns: "Found these documents: Maintenance Schedule, Lab Access Plan, Vendor Notes." What is the main design problem?
options: The response is too short to be useful
The tone is too informal for a tool result
No identifiers or structured fields — downstream tools can't act on prose titles, and the agent must guess how to reference each document
It should have returned the full text of each document
rationales: Length isn't the issue — the same information structured would be excellent.
Tools aren't judged on tone; they're judged on machine-usability.
Tool results should be structured and carry the IDs the next decision needs (`document_id`, owner, updated_at). Prose-only output forces fragile title-matching downstream.
Returning full text wastes tokens; results should be compact and useful for the next decision.

stem: A tool wraps an API whose queries can match thousands of records. How should it handle result volume?
options: Fetch all pages so the agent has complete information
Return the first page plus a `total_count` and a cursor, fetching more only when actually needed
Return only the count and require a second call for any data
Cap results at 10 and silently drop the rest
rationales: Auto-fetching everything burns latency and tokens and can blow the context on large matches — usually for items nobody needed.
First page + total + cursor gives the agent enough to decide whether more is worth fetching. Volume control stays a deliberate choice.
Forcing two calls for every query adds a turn to the common case.
Silent truncation misrepresents the result set; the agent can't know more exists.

stem: An agent platform has 80 connectors' worth of operations. Exposing them all degrades tool selection. Which design fixes this without hiding decisions from the agent?
options: Group operations into ten mega-tools with an `operation` parameter
Keep all 80 exposed but sort the most-used first
Add a single `find_and_execute(task)` tool that searches for and runs the best operation
Discovery tools return a ranked shortlist of relevant operations, which are then dynamically added to the agent's available tools for subsequent turns
rationales: Mega-tools recreate the free-form `action` parameter problem — schemas can no longer encode per-operation requirements.
Ordering doesn't reduce the choice set; selection accuracy still degrades with count.
Search-and-execute hides the final decision inside one call and can perform the wrong action before anyone can inspect it.
Progressive availability narrows choices while keeping execution a separate, inspectable step — the pattern the Agent SDK supports natively via tool search and dynamic registration.
sourceRef: ## 2 > Large Tool Sets and Progressive Availability

stem: An ML-backed extraction tool returns per-field confidence scores. How should it help the agent decide what needs human review?
options: Return the data plus a derived `requires_review` boolean with reasons, using thresholds calibrated against a labeled validation set
Return raw confidence scores and let the model judge each one
Round confidences to high/medium/low so the model has fewer values to reason about
Suppress fields below 0.9 confidence entirely
rationales: Calibrated thresholds turn statistical scores into consistent decisions; the boolean plus reasons gives the agent an actionable signal instead of an interpretation problem.
Raw scores invite both over-trust and over-escalation — the model has no basis for knowing what 0.62 means for this pipeline.
Coarser buckets are still uncalibrated; the interpretation burden remains.
A hard cutoff hides potentially correct data and still embeds an uncalibrated threshold.

stem: A workspace-deletion operation must always be previewed by a human before executing. Which design actually enforces that?
options: A `dry_run: true` default the model can override when the user seems sure
A system prompt rule: "always preview deletions before executing"
`preview_delete_workspace` returns the impact and a one-time confirmation token; `execute_delete_workspace` requires that token and verifies it matches the previewed action
Logging every deletion for weekly audit
rationales: The model controls the flag, so nothing structurally prevents `dry_run: false` on the first call.
Prompt rules are probabilistic; a must-always-hold invariant needs structural enforcement.
The two-tool token pattern makes execution *impossible* without a preview having happened — the sequence is enforced by the interface, not by model compliance.
Audit finds violations after the workspace is gone.

stem: An agent can already run Bash. Why promote email-sending to a dedicated `send_email` tool anyway?
options: Bash cannot make network calls in agent environments
A dedicated tool can be gated behind confirmation, rendered specially, audited, and safely parallelized — `bash -c "curl -X POST ..."` gives the application only an opaque string to inspect
Dedicated tools execute faster than shell commands
The model writes more reliable curl commands when a tool with the same name exists
rationales: Bash typically can make network calls — that's exactly the problem.

The heuristic: promote an action to a dedicated tool when the application needs to intercept it. A structured `send_email` call can pass through approval and audit paths; an opaque command string cannot.
Speed isn't the distinction; interceptability is.
Tool existence doesn't improve unrelated shell commands.
sourceRef: ## 2 > Where Tools Run (Bash vs dedicated tool)

stem: Which pair of composition decisions is correct for (1) a booking system where slots can be taken between calls, and (2) a research workflow that retrieves sources and writes conclusions?
options: (1) Combine availability-check and reserve into one atomic operation — separate calls create a race; (2) keep retrieval and conclusion-writing separate — the model must inspect sources and preserve provenance
(1) Keep check and reserve separate for auditability; (2) combine retrieve-and-conclude for efficiency
Combine both — fewer calls is always better
Separate both — composition is an anti-pattern
rationales: Composition is for mechanical sequences and atomicity needs; separation is for judgment. Booking needs atomicity (the race is real); research needs the model between retrieval and writing.
Backwards on both counts: the separate booking calls are exactly what creates the race, and merging research steps hides required judgment.
Combining the research steps removes the inspection and provenance step the task needs.
Atomic check-and-book is a legitimate, sometimes necessary composition.

stem: A neighborhood-info tool requires an address, so agents always call `get_property_details(property_id)` first just to extract the address and pass it along. What is the better design?
options: Cache the address after the first lookup to speed up later calls
Combine both tools into one `get_property_with_neighborhood` call
Have the system prompt remind the agent to fetch details first
Have the neighborhood tool accept `property_id` directly and resolve the address internally — the intermediate hop is mechanical, requiring no model judgment
rationales: Caching speeds the workaround instead of removing it.
Merging couples two capabilities that are separately useful; the actual problem is just the parameter choice.
Prompt guidance institutionalizes the unnecessary hop.
Prefer stable identifiers over derived intermediates: when a dependency is mechanical, the tool should resolve it internally, eliminating a lookup's latency and its failure coupling.
